title: Hey guys, I'm new to all of this, love

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: I've seen this, problem is it's very high level e.g. the serial connection gets established here and that hides a lot of complexity in terms of parity bits, flow control, etc. and the python code uses high level constructs like asyncio with coroutines and tasks, too. Thought maybe we have something a bit more low level for core functionality to get started with.
messages.content_html: I've seen this, problem is it's very high level e.g. the serial connection gets established here and that hides a lot of complexity in terms of parity bits, flow control, etc. and the python code uses high level constructs like asyncio with coroutines and tasks, too. Thought maybe we have something a bit more low level for core functionality to get started with.

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: there's also pyMC_Core
messages.content_html: there's also pyMC_Core

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: there's also the nodejs lib I wrote: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore.js/tree/master/examples https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore.js/blob/master/src/connection/connection.js
messages.content_html: there's also the nodejs lib I wrote: https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore.js/tree/master/examples https://github.com/meshcore-dev/meshcore.js/blob/master/src/connection/connection.js
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: Yess they're great and I'm reading through the py and js code right now. What I meant was e.g. the low level protocol that's hard to reconstruct from those libs e.g. 1. When talking via usb/serial can we assume for every command we get exactly one response? I have seen push responses, does it mean on the line we can have: cmd1 (out), resp1 (in), resp2 (in). And the response for cmd1 might be resp2 and not directly after the command? 2. Packets on the usb/serial wire seem to be length delimited, does that mean we need to read incoming bytes until we get a "<"/">" byte, then read the length, and then the rest? Are there any guarantees that we are only sending packets on the wire so that I can simply assume the first byte read is "<" or ">"? 3. Is the usb/serial connection full duplex where I can read and write at the same time? Or do I need to first write a packet, wait for the serial connection to flush it, and then read a response fully?
messages.content_html: Yess they're great and I'm reading through the py and js code right now. What I meant was e.g. the low level protocol that's hard to reconstruct from those libs e.g. 1. When talking via usb/serial can we assume for every command we get exactly one response? I have seen push responses, does it mean on the line we can have: cmd1 (out), resp1 (in), resp2 (in). And the response for cmd1 might be resp2 and not directly after the command? 2. Packets on the usb/serial wire seem to be length delimited, does that mean we need to read incoming bytes until we get a "<"/">" byte, then read the length, and then the rest? Are there any guarantees that we are only sending packets on the wire so that I can simply assume the first byte read is "<" or ">"? 3. Is the usb/serial connection full duplex where I can read and write at the same time? Or do I need to first write a packet, wait for the serial connection to flush it, and then read a response fully?

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: What are your project goals? There might be better options than using companion firmware, depending on what those are. MeshTNC, for example, might be a better fit if you're interested in sending meshcore packets
messages.content_html: What are your project goals? There might be better options than using companion firmware, depending on what those are. MeshTNC, for example, might be a better fit if you're interested in sending meshcore packets

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: Really just experimenting and learning at this point. I'm very new to all of this and wanted to dig a bit deeper in my free evenings and weekends. I have a T1000-E and a Heltec T114v2 that I use to play around and just joined the Berlin meshcore usergroup meetup for the first time, seems like good fun!
messages.content_html: Really just experimenting and learning at this point. I'm very new to all of this and wanted to dig a bit deeper in my free evenings and weekends. I have a T1000-E and a Heltec T114v2 that I use to play around and just joined the Berlin meshcore usergroup meetup for the first time, seems like good fun!
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: Hey guys I got a veery first version going, it's very basic but self-contained and helped me understand the serial protocol a bit better and a few edge cases in there. Maybe it helps anyone: https://github.com/daniel-j-h/cmeshcore What I found a bit tricky is the the async nature of those pushed frames and the serial communication handling e.g. partial reads and retries and all that in a low level language. Otherwise pretty solid stuff!!
messages.content_html: Hey guys I got a veery first version going, it's very basic but self-contained and helped me understand the serial protocol a bit better and a few edge cases in there. Maybe it helps anyone: https://github.com/daniel-j-h/cmeshcore What I found a bit tricky is the the async nature of those pushed frames and the serial communication handling e.g. partial reads and retries and all that in a low level language. Otherwise pretty solid stuff!!

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: in a future companion protocol update, I'd love to see request/response sequence tagging, so you know for sure which reply packet is for which command/request
messages.content_html: in a future companion protocol update, I'd love to see request/response sequence tagging, so you know for sure which reply packet is for which command/request
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: Isn't it already in there?
messages.content_html: Isn't it already in there?

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: for the lora packet side yes, not for the companion protocol side
messages.content_html: for the lora packet side yes, not for the companion protocol side
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: But, I'd like to request adding a flag to the response so we know how to parse it when we don't have the request packet.
messages.content_html: But, I'd like to request adding a flag to the response so we know how to parse it when we don't have the request packet.

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: Not too sure what you mean by this one
messages.content_html: Not too sure what you mean by this one
messages.reply_html:
hpux735
But, I'd like to request adding a flag to the response so we know how to parse it when we don't have the request packet.

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: the respose packets are only a timestamp/tag then data. There's no way to know whether the data is a stats, telemetry, ACL, or neighbors packet. Only if you have the request packet with the matching tag do you know. I'd like to have one byte, after the timestamp/tag, that tells you what kind of response it is
messages.content_html: the respose packets are only a timestamp/tag then data. There's no way to know whether the data is a stats, telemetry, ACL, or neighbors packet. Only if you have the request packet with the matching tag do you know. I'd like to have one byte, after the timestamp/tag, that tells you what kind of response it is

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: ah right. yeah there's no response type at the start
messages.content_html: ah right. yeah there's no response type at the start

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: it makes making a protocol dissector very challenging
messages.content_html: it makes making a protocol dissector very challenging

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: I think that was done since it wasn't really expected to use the response in any other context, than for the purpose of a response to the request you sent
messages.content_html: I think that was done since it wasn't really expected to use the response in any other context, than for the purpose of a response to the request you sent

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: Yah, I understand that. But stateful protocols aren't very fun
messages.content_html: Yah, I understand that. But stateful protocols aren't very fun

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: should probably take that into consideration for future request/response types that are added (edited)
messages.content_html: should probably take that into consideration for future request/response types that are added (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: Scott
messages.text: Only the requester is supposed to know how to interpret the response.
messages.content_html: Only the requester is supposed to know how to interpret the response.
messages.reactions_html: 1
messages.reply_html:
Liam Cottle
^ @Scott

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: That's not great when you're making network debugging and analysis tools. :/
messages.content_html: That's not great when you're making network debugging and analysis tools. :/
messages.reply_html:
Scott
Only the requester is supposed to know how to interpret the response.

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: Hey so I'm noticing that when sending a text message with CMD_SEND_TXT_MSG that the message I can send is shorter than the 160 bytes as documented on https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/wiki/Companion-Radio-Protocol Is it possible that it's 160 bytes minus a few bytes? And a follow up question: Is there a way to send more than these 160'ish bytes and where is this limitation coming from? I've also noticed that for the message command and responses CMD_SEND_TXT_MSG RESP_CODE_CONTACT_MSG_RECV RESP_CODE_CONTACT_MSG_RECV_V3 we get a 6-bytes public key prefix; what's the behavior when there are two nodes with the same 6-bytes prefix but different remaining 32-6=26 bytes?
messages.content_html: Hey so I'm noticing that when sending a text message with CMD_SEND_TXT_MSG that the message I can send is shorter than the 160 bytes as documented on https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/wiki/Companion-Radio-Protocol Is it possible that it's 160 bytes minus a few bytes? And a follow up question: Is there a way to send more than these 160'ish bytes and where is this limitation coming from? I've also noticed that for the message command and responses CMD_SEND_TXT_MSG RESP_CODE_CONTACT_MSG_RECV RESP_CODE_CONTACT_MSG_RECV_V3 we get a 6-bytes public key prefix; what's the behavior when there are two nodes with the same 6-bytes prefix but different remaining 32-6=26 bytes?

messages.author: hpux735
messages.text: the prefix is just used as a filter to limit the number of public keys to check when you decode the message. The MAC is what's really used to determine the sender
messages.content_html: the prefix is just used as a filter to limit the number of public keys to check when you decode the message. The MAC is what's really used to determine the sender

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: Got it, that makes sense. Was just wondering why it's a prefix here but in other protocol parts it's the full 32 bytes public key.
messages.content_html: Got it, that makes sense. Was just wondering why it's a prefix here but in other protocol parts it's the full 32 bytes public key.

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: Regarding the private/channel message limit, where can I find the exact limit of bytes I can transfer per message and is there a way to send more than the ~160 something bytes? I also started looking into how to backup the private key for a device via serial, and looks like there is no command in the wiki docs; can it be that it's missing there? https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/wiki/Companion-Radio-Protocol
